metadata.title: Why the Average Particle Stops Behaving Classically
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = narrow packet (follows classical force, expectation value = actual position); CRIMSON = wide packet (splits into two lobes, expectation value stranded at center)
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no derivation of Ehrenfest equations, no Heisenberg-picture operator algebra, no V''' error-term math.

beats.narration_text: The center of a quantum wave packet obeys Newton's laws — right up until the packet gets fat, and then the 'average position' describes a place the particle is never actually found.

card.copy: Why the Average Particle Stops Behaving Classically

beats.narration_text: Ehrenfest's theorem says the expectation value of position evolves just like a classical particle: the average moves at the average velocity, and the average velocity changes according to the average force. For a narrow packet in a smooth potential, this works perfectly.

Place the packet in a double well: two minima with a central hill. A narrow packet rolls down to one minimum like a ball would. The expectation value tracks its position faithfully. Classical and quantum agree.

The Question: Ehrenfest says the average position follows the average force. In a double well, a wide packet splits into two lobes over the two minima — but its average sits on the central hilltop where the particle is never found. What went wrong?

card.copy: The average position should follow the average force. In a double well, the wide packet's average gets stranded where the particle never is. Why?

beats.narration_text: The naive reading: the expectation value should track where the particle is most likely to be. If the packet splits to the left and right minima, the average should follow whichever lobe is bigger. It should never be stranded at a hilltop where probability is zero.

Ehrenfest's equation computes the average force as the expectation value of minus dV over dx — a weighted average over the whole wave function. When the packet is narrow, the force barely varies across it, so the average force equals the force at the average position. Classical mechanics applies.

But when the packet is wide — spread across the whole double well — the force at the left lobe points right, and the force at the right lobe points left. The average of these two opposing forces is nearly zero. The average position sits at the center, pushed by nearly nothing.

The expectation value is a mathematical average over the whole distribution. It is not where the particle is. When the distribution has two widely separated humps, the average sits between them — at a location with zero probability. The average description and the actual description have separated.

The implication: quantum mechanics does NOT say particles follow classical paths. It says the average of a narrow packet follows a classical path. Get the packet fat enough — or put it in a potential that splits it — and the average becomes meaningless as a description of the particle.

A physicist calculates the average position of a particle trapped in a double well. She starts narrow: the average tracks the particle to the left minimum. She starts wide: the average stalls at the center hilltop. She reports both results. They are both correct — but only the narrow one tells her where the particle actually is.
beats.example_note: illustrative — narrow vs wide packet in a double well, tracking expectation value

beats.narration_text: Why does the average particle stop behaving classically? Ehrenfest says the average follows the average force — and the average force is not the force at the average position. Split the packet across a double well and the two lobes push in opposite directions. The average stalls at the hilltop. The average is real, but the particle is not there.

card.copy: Average force is not force at average position. Wide packet in a double well: lobes push opposite ways. Average stalls at a place the particle never is.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom series.
